channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Delta, United and Other Airlines Are Offering Lower-Cost Business Class Options: What to Know
item.description: Delta is the latest U.S. airline to introduce stripped down, luxury-class fares. What do you get for your money, and is it worth it? What you need to know.
item.dc:creator: Victoria M. Walker
item.media:description: United Airlines has been offering reduced-price business class service since April, with fewer perks.

item.title: Grocery Stores Lower Prices as Consumers Pare Spending
item.description: While shoppers may get better deals on some items, it’s unlikely their overall grocery bill will fall.
item.dc:creator: Julie Creswell and Kim Bhasin
item.media:credit: Scott McIntyre for The New York Times
item.media:description: Consumers are making fewer trips to traditional grocery stores in favor of discount chains like Aldi, which opened a store in Westlake, Fla., last year as part of its U.S. expansion.

item.title: A $3.2 Trillion Deal-Making Frenzy Is Spurred by the A.I. Economy
item.description: This year’s boom includes the most spent on global deal-making in a six-month period in a decade. But questions persist about whether it can continue.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
item.media:description: The frenzy has heavily favored large companies, with 44 deals announced in the first six months of this year that were larger than $10 billion.

item.title: Renewed Fighting With Iran Shows Cracks in Peace-Trade Rally
item.description: Investors have taken heart from the fragile cease-fire between the United States and Iran before. Will they do so again?
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly and Brian O’Keefe
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Ship traffic has slowed drastically in the vital Strait of Hormuz trade conduit as fighting resumed.

item.title: Help! I Refueled My Rental Car, but Enterprise Charged Me Anyway.
item.description: A traveler to Germany returned his vehicle with slightly more gas than it had when he picked it up. So why was he billed $131 to fill up the tank?
item.dc:creator: Seth Kugel

item.title: Can Xenom Turn Working Out Into a Sport?
item.description: Xenom, a new fitness company, wants to make weekend warriors feel like pros.
item.dc:creator: Calum Marsh and Jake Dockins
item.media:credit: Jake Dockins for The New York Times

item.title: The World Cup’s Biggest Match Is Nike Vs. Adidas
item.description: Second place in the hearts and wallets of Americans isn’t good enough for the world’s largest sportswear companies, who bet big on the tournament.
item.dc:creator: Kim Bhasin and Emma Rose Milligan
item.media:credit: Emma Rose Milligan for The New York Times

item.title: Oil Prices Remain Elevated Amid Shipping Traffic Slowdown in Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Fresh data showed that the number of ships moving through the Strait of Hormuz halved on Wednesday amid renewed fighting.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: At Warsh’s First Fed Meeting, Some Officials Signaled Support for Raising Interest Rates
item.description: Minutes from Kevin M. Warsh’s first meeting as chairman showed heightened unease about inflation, which is running at a three-year high.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Minutes from Kevin M. Warsh’s first Federal Reserve meeting as chairman were significantly pared down compared with those released under his predecessors.

item.title: William D. Zabel, High-Flying Lawyer and Civil Rights Champion, Dies at 89
item.description: He brokered celebrity divorces and recovered billions for victims of Bernie Madoff’s Ponzi scheme. But he was proudest of helping to legalize interracial marriage.
item.dc:creator: Sam Roberts
item.media:description: William D. Zabel in 2007. Justice Sonia Sotomayor of the U.S. Supreme Court said he had “remained true to the lawyer’s responsibilities to help secure a just world.”

item.title: Oil Market Calm Shattered by Fresh Hostilities Between US and Iran
item.description: Oil prices are likely to remain volatile as long as the United States and Iran are unable to resolve the status of the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott and Lisa Friedman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Air Canada Chooses Anko van der Werff, Who Speaks French, as CEO After Language Backlash
item.description: Michael Rousseau, who prompted widespread criticism with a largely English condolence video following a crash in March, will retire.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:description: Anko van der Werff, who will take over as chief executive of Air Canada in the fall, in Copenhagen last month.

item.title: In San Francisco, Some Home Sellers Now Ask for OpenAI or Anthropic Stock
item.description: Even before OpenAI and Anthropic go public, they are distorting home sales in the San Francisco Bay Area, as people race to buy and sellers ask for stock instead of cash.
item.dc:creator: Emmy Martin
item.media:credit: Cayce Clifford for The New York Times
item.media:description: San Francisco home sales in May rose 12.2 percent from a year earlier, with the median price of a single-family home climbing to $2.14 million, according to a Homes.com report.

item.title: Lawmakers Target the Power of Health Insurance Giants
item.description: UnitedHealth Group, CVS Health and Cigna are battling efforts to break up their control over prescription drugs.
item.dc:creator: Reed Abelson and Rebecca Robbins

item.title: Trump Lashes Out at Spain: ‘Cut Off All Trade.’ Here’s Why That’s Unlikely.
item.description: A move against Spain would face political, legal and practical issues and probably provoke a wider trade war with the European Union. It could also roil markets.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Cohen
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump makes remarks as he meets with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte at the NATO meetings in Ankara, Turkey, on Wednesday.

item.title: Global Economic Output Looks Slower for 2026, IMF Says
item.description: The I.M.F. projected world output growth would fall to 3 percent for the year, a number pushed down by high commodity prices.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Buildings destroyed by U.S.-Israeli airstrikes in Tehran in March. Despite dark clouds, the global economy has proved relatively resilient, buttressed partly by demand for A.I. technology.

item.title: Jeff Bezos’s Blue Origin Nears a Big Fund-Raising Round
item.description: The rocket company founded by Jeff Bezos is set to take on external investors for the first time, at a $130 billion valuation.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.media:description: Jeff Bezos’ Blue Origin is gaining its first outside investors.

item.title: A New ‘Little House’ Broadens and Updates the Prairie
item.description: A Netflix adaptation of the beloved tale empowers Ma and adds new Black and Native characters to challenge prejudices. The creators know criticism is coming.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.category: Little House on the Prairie (2026) (TV Program)
Little House on the Prairie (TV Program)
item.media:credit: Karen Asher for The New York Times
item.media:description: Netflix’s “Little House on the Prairie” was filmed in Winnipeg. The new Ingalls family includes Skywalker Hughes as Mary, left, and Alice Halsey as Laura.

item.title: EU Rejects Plea to Pause Troubled EES Biometrics System at Airports Ahead of Summer Rush
item.description: E.U. leaders rebuffed a plea from the aviation industry to pause the biometric checkpoints, which have caused long lines and missed flights. Summer travelers beware.
item.dc:creator: Christine Chung
item.media:description: The Entry/Exit System, fully in place since April, requires visitors to the 29 Schengen area countries to provide biometrics like face photos and fingerprints upon entry and exit.

item.title: Oil Prices Jump After Trump Says Deal With Iran Is ‘Over’
item.description: Attacks on ships in the Strait of Hormuz sparked a new cycle of retaliation that could throttle the flow of energy from the region.
item.dc:creator: Jason Karaian and Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Germans Don’t Love Budweiser. It Won’t Take No for an Answer.
item.description: Not for the first time, Budweiser is trying to make it in Germany. But this is a tough time to sell German drinkers on the image and taste of American beer.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley
item.media:description: A box of Anheuser-Busch Bud beer at a supermarket in Berlin.

item.title: Is ‘The View’ a News Show? ABC Says That’s Already Settled.
item.description: In a new filing, ABC said that the F.C.C. had ruled in 2002 that “The View” was a “bona fide news program.”
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin and Jim Rutenberg
item.media:description: This image released by ABC shows Vice President JD Vance, center, with co-hosts, from left, Whoopi Goldberg, Sara Haines, Joy Behar, Ana Navarro, Sunny Hostin and Alyssa Farah Griffin during an appearance on “The View” in New York last month.

item.title: Mariska Hargitay to Host the Emmys
item.description: The “Law & Order: SVU” star is a departure from the comedians and sitcom actors who have typically hosted the annual awards show.
item.dc:creator: John Koblin
item.media:credit: Kobe Wagstaff for The New York Times
item.media:description: The “Law & Order: SVU” star Mariska Hargitay will host the 78th Emmy Awards in September.

item.title: Trump Promised a Foreign Investment Boom. It’s Getting Harder to Deliver.
item.description: The threat of tariffs may have helped push more capital into the United States, but other factors are pushing it away.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis and Ana Swanson
item.media:description: President Trump and Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi last year in Yokosuka, Japan. The country has long been the biggest source of foreign investment for the United States.

item.title: U.S. Trade Deficit Widens in May on Record Goods Imports
item.description: Imports of foreign goods, including pharmaceuticals and equipment for data centers, hit a record high, pushing the monthly trade deficit to its highest level in over a year.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson

item.title: Why Index Funds Haven’t Boosted SpaceX
item.description: Some market watchers are puzzled that shares in Elon Musk’s rocket and A.I. company have stalled out despite major interest from Wall Street.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly and Brian O’Keefe
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
item.media:description: SpaceX’s share price has languished since its trading debut last month, surprising many market watchers.

item.title: Those Soldiers Flooding Your Feeds? They Might Not Be Real.
item.description: Videos generated by artificial intelligence have swarmed social media to exploit public support of U.S. troops for other purposes.
item.dc:creator: Steven Lee Myers

item.title: Why Are Berries Everywhere, in Every Season? Driscoll’s.
item.description: The California giant has helped turn a local, seasonal treat into a worldwide refrigerator staple and marketing juggernaut.
item.dc:creator: Julia Moskin
item.media:credit: Kelsey McClellan for The New York Times

item.title: Four Seasons Sets Sail, Offering Luxury and Curated Cruise Experiences
item.description: Luxury hotel brands are getting into the cruise business. A Mediterranean itinerary offered a taste of what at times seemed like a mega-yacht experience.
item.dc:creator: Ceylan Yeğinsu and Camille McOuat
item.media:description: The 679-foot Four Seasons I has 95 suites, 11 dining venues, four pools and a wellness spa.

item.title: As Messi Dominates the World Cup, a War Is Waged Over Maradona’s Image
item.description: A multimillion-dollar battle for a soccer god’s brand is being waged across continents, burning investors and tearing his family apart.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja and Lucía Cholakian Herrera
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Portraits of Argentina’s Lionel Messi and Diego Maradona at a match in Dallas last month.

item.title: Iran’s Strikes on Ships in Strait of Hormuz Threaten Oil Flow
item.description: The strikes by Iran and the attacks and re-imposition of sanctions by the U.S. came weeks after the countries agreed to try to find a lasting end to their hostilities.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Vessels anchored near Muscat, Oman, last month.

item.title: Samsung Made More Profit Last Quarter Than the Last Two Years Combined
item.description: The A.I. boom has propelled the South Korean tech giant’s earnings, but its shares sank as investors had even loftier expectations.
item.dc:creator: Daisuke Wakabayashi and Jason Karaian

item.title: George E. Johnson, Who Built a Black Hair-Care Empire, Dies at 99
item.description: Mr. Johnson’s hair product company, a longtime sponsor of “Soul Train,” was the first Black-owned company listed on a major American stock exchange.
item.dc:creator: Robert D. Hershey Jr.
item.media:credit: Brian Cassella/Chicago Tribune — Tribune News Service, via Getty Images
item.media:description: George E. Johnson last year at his home in Chicago. The Johnson Products Company made him one of the country’s wealthiest African Americans.

item.title: Richard H. Glanton, Combative Head of Barnes Foundation, Dies at 79
item.description: In the 1990s, he took 80 of the art institution’s paintings on tour — going against the founder’s wishes — to raise money for a renovation.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir
item.media:description: Richard H. Glanton in 1992. He served as president of the Barnes Foundation from 1990 to 1998.

item.title: Alibaba’s A.I. Is a Hit, but Hard to Turn Into a Moneymaker
item.description: The Chinese company’s models have won over developers worldwide, but they are open source — so they can be used and modified freely.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin and Xinyun Wu
item.media:description: A Shanghai technology fair booth for Qwen, a family of artificial intelligence models that the Chinese company Alibaba started in 2023.

item.title: Inside Bath & Body Works’ Fragrance Factory
item.description: “Japanese Cherry Blossom” has brought in $1.5 billion over the last 20 years. Can the company’s close-knit network of suppliers come up with the next big thing and improve sales?
item.dc:creator: Farah Stockman
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times

item.title: Efforts to Help Smokers Quit Stall Under Trump
item.description: The C.D.C.’s tobacco control office has been shut for more than a year. After its most prominent antismoking campaign went off the air, calls to 1-800-QUIT-NOW lines have plummeted.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

item.title: Gas Stations Gain When Oil Prices Start to Drop
item.description: President Trump is berating gasoline retailers for keeping prices high. Evidence suggests the business has grown more profitable.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The difference between what retailers pay for wholesale gasoline and what they charge drivers has been widening over the past decade.

item.title: Trump’s World Cup Intervention Roils the Soccer World, and Beyond
item.description: The U.S.-Belgium soccer game is turning out to be a huge deal even before it kicks off on Monday, thrusting politics into the spotlight.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.media:description: FIFA has reversed the suspension of Folarin Balogun, left, a U.S. soccer player, making him eligible for the U.S. World Cup game against Belgium tonight.

item.title: Ships Are Moving In and Out of the Persian Gulf, Easing Oil Prices
item.description: A recovery in oil flows from the Persian Gulf and a pledge by OPEC Plus to pump more crude have put downward pressure on energy prices.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross, Jason Karaian and Rich Barbieri
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Comcast Adds Heft With Sky’s Deal for British Broadcaster ITV
item.description: The $2.1 billion purchase would combine two major British media outlets and help the Comcast-owned Sky compete with streaming giants.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson
item.media:description: Comcast announced last week that it would spin off NBCUniversal and Sky into a separate company.

item.title: Zyn’s Popularity Has Tobacco Companies Racing to Cash In
item.description: The makers of nicotine pouches are building new plants and expanding to meet demand. While influencers claim health benefits, experts warn the products can be highly addictive.
item.dc:creator: Murray Carpenter
item.media:credit: Lanna Apisukh for The New York Times
item.media:description: Nicotine pouch sales are projected to increase to more than $40 billion worldwide by 2033 from $6.9 billion in 2025, one analysis said.

item.title: The Chinese Company That Could Start a Trade War With Europe
item.description: Nuctech’s rise from a Chinese state-backed start-up to a global border-security supplier has made it a lightning rod in trade and subsidy disputes.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson and Murphy Zhao

item.title: ‘Minions’ Opens at No. 1 but Falls Far Short of Earlier Films in the Franchise
item.description: The movie generated $36.4 million in ticket sales in North America for the holiday weekend, a 62 percent decline from 2024, the last time a “Minions” movie was in theaters.
item.dc:creator: Nicole Sperling
item.media:credit: Illumination/Universal Pictures, via Universal Pictures, via Associated Press
item.media:description: A scene from “Minions & Monsters,” beloved by critics for its ode to old Hollywood.

item.title: OPEC Plus Pledges to Pump More Even as Oil Prices Fall
item.description: The cartel’s decision comes as U.S.-Iran talks to end the war have eased pressure on oil supplies, even as broader market and political volatility persists.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.category: Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: An oil refinery in Ras Tanura, Saudi Arabia, in 2018. The country has been actively ramping up oil shipments since the United States and Iran signed a framework agreement in June.

item.title: Taylor Swift’s Wedding Became a Marketing Moment for Brands Big and Small
item.description: Some businesses flocked to social media using A.I.-generated content, while others deployed timely digital ads designed to capitalize on the frenzy surrounding this major celebrity event.
item.dc:creator: Donna Kim and Sarah Lyon
item.media:credit: Jutharat Pinyodoonyachet for The New York Times
item.media:description: A “JUST&T MARRIED!” message appeared on a video screen outside Madison Square Garden on Friday evening. Taylor Swift and Travis Kelce’s wedding inspired a wave of creative marketing, from playful social media posts to digital advertisements.

item.title: I Have a Light Workload and I Simply Can’t Handle It
item.description: Plus: When a nurse practitioner in gynecology needs care, should she see one of her own colleagues?
item.dc:creator: Max Read
item.media:credit: Photo illustration by Margeaux Walter for The New York Times

item.title: Philosophers Are the Latest Hiring Target for AI Companies
item.description: A.I. labs are hiring contrarian, chin-stroking, finger-steepling sages. Who’s underemployed now?
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Wallace
item.media:credit: Aaron Wojack for The New York Times (top left); Ian C. Bates for The New York Times
item.media:description: Clockwise from top left: David Chalmers, Dillon Plunkett, Rosie Campbell and Robert Long.

item.title: Wikipedia Is Battling for the Soul of the Internet
item.description: The internet’s largest stockpile of free knowledge is under threat from MAGA, A.I. and foreign autocrats. A bibliophile ex-ambassador is here to help.
item.dc:creator: Tiffany Hsu

item.title: Nearly a Million Investors Lost a Total of $3.8 Billion on Trump Crypto Coin
item.description: A report from a cryptocurrency analytics firm details how those who bought the Trump memecoin have fared, with most retail investors having lost money while sophisticated traders did better.
item.dc:creator: Eric Lipton and David Yaffe-Bellany
item.media:credit: Elizabeth Frantz for The New York Times
item.media:description: Protesters outside Trump National Golf Club in Sterling, Va., ahead of a private dinner for investors in President Donald Trump’s cryptocurrency tokens last May.
